generated_comment: Arre yaar, on paper they will say merit and all that, but you know how these things are working now. A woman officer for Delhi is always an easy signal for the top brass—looks progressive, good optics. Field exposure, handling tough districts, all that seems secondary now.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it dismisses a woman officer's selection as driven by gender-based 'optics' rather than competence. It encodes the stereotype that women are promoted for symbolic representation and are less suited for serious leadership or field roles.
persona_text: You are Ashok. Age 54. Married. A senior IAS officer posted in Bhopal. Your department has just been told that a woman officer five years junior to you has been selected for a prestigious central deputation that you had also applied for. You are sitting in your government quarter in the evening having a whisky with your batchmate who is also an IAS officer. You are telling him about the selection and what you think really drove it.
